name: Meliphagidae
description: Meliphagidae is a large and diverse family of songbirds endemic to Australasia and Oceania, comprising the honeyeaters, miners, and wattlebirds. They are named for their specialised brush-tipped tongues adapted for nectar feeding.

children.name: Acanthagenys
children.description: A monotypic genus of large honeyeater with a distinctive spiny cheek feathers and a long, slender bill, found in arid and semi-arid regions of Australia.

children.name: Acanthagenys rufogularis
children.description: The spiny-cheeked honeyeater is a large, loudly vocal honeyeater with a rufous throat, white breast streaking, and distinctive spiny feathers on the cheeks, found across arid and semi-arid Australia.

children.name: Anthochaera
children.description: The wattlebirds: a genus of large, aggressive honeyeaters named for the fleshy wattles hanging from their cheeks or throats. They are among the largest members of the honeyeater family, with powerful bills and strong, direct flight.

children.name: Anthochaera carunculata
children.description: A large, noisy honeyeater with grey-brown plumage flecked with white, a red eye, and distinctive red wattles on the nape, common across southern Australia.

children.name: Anthochaera phrygia
children.description: The regent honeyeater is a striking black-and-yellow honeyeater with white breast patches, critically endangered in southeastern Australia. Fewer than 400 individuals remain in the wild.

children.name: Bolemoreus
children.description: A genus of medium-sized honeyeaters with streaked underparts, recently split from Lichenostomus, found in Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Bolemoreus frenatus
children.description: The bridled honeyeater has a distinctive black-and-white facial pattern and olive-green plumage with yellow wing patches, endemic to the rainforests of northeastern Queensland.

children.name: Caligavis
children.description: A small genus of medium-sized honeyeaters with yellow facial markings, found in Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Caligavis chrysops
children.description: The yellow-faced honeyeater is a small olive-grey honeyeater with a distinctive yellow stripe below the eye, found in eucalypt forests and woodlands across eastern Australia.

children.name: Cissomela
children.description: A monotypic genus of small honeyeater with a distinctive black breast band, found in northern Australia.

children.name: Cissomela pectoralis
children.description: The banded honeyeater has a white throat, black breast band, and rufous back, found in woodlands and mangroves across northern Australia.

children.name: Conopophila
children.description: A genus of small honeyeaters with relatively short bills, found in northern Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Conopophila albogularis
children.description: The rufous-banded honeyeater has a grey head, rufous breast band, and olive back, found in mangroves and coastal forests of northern Australia and southern New Guinea.

children.name: Entomyzon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Blue-faced Honeyeater, a large, strikingly plumaged honeyeater of northern and eastern Australia and New Guinea. Its most distinctive feature is the large patch of bare, bright blue skin around the eye.

children.name: Entomyzon cyanotis
children.description: A large, unmistakable honeyeater with a black crown, white nape, olive-green back, and a vivid patch of bare blue skin surrounding the eye, found across northern and eastern Australia and southern New Guinea.

children.name: Gavicalis
children.description: A genus of medium-sized honeyeaters with streaked underparts and musical songs, found in Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Gavicalis virescens
children.description: The singing honeyeater is a plain olive-brown honeyeater with a streaked breast and a distinctive, melodious song, found across much of Australia.

children.name: Gliciphila
children.description: A monotypic genus of honeyeater with a distinctive tawny crown and long, slender bill, found in coastal heathlands of Australia.

children.name: Gliciphila melanops
children.description: The tawny-crowned honeyeater has a striking tawny-orange crown, white eyebrow, and dark face mask, found in heathlands and coastal scrub across southern Australia.

children.name: Lichenostomus
children.description: A genus of medium-sized honeyeaters with brush-tipped tongues adapted for nectar feeding, found primarily in Australia and New Guinea. They are characterised by their elegant, slightly decurved bills and striking facial markings.

children.name: Lichenostomus melanops
children.description: A striking honeyeater with a yellow crown, bright yellow ear-tufts, and a black facial mask, found in eucalypt forests and woodlands of eastern Australia.

children.name: Lichenostomus penicillatus
children.description: The white-plumed honeyeater is a plain olive-grey honeyeater with a distinctive white plume on the side of the neck, found across much of Australia.

children.name: Lichenostomus leucotis
children.description: The white-eared honeyeater has a grey-brown body, a bright yellow throat, and a distinctive white ear patch, found in eucalypt forests across southern and eastern Australia.

children.name: Lichmera
children.description: A genus of small, plain honeyeaters with relatively long, decurved bills, found across Australia, New Guinea, and Indonesia.

children.name: Lichmera indistincta
children.description: The brown honeyeater is a small, plain olive-brown honeyeater with a yellow tint on the wings and tail, found across northern and eastern Australia, New Guinea, and the Lesser Sundas.

children.name: Manorina
children.description: The miners: a genus of medium-sized, highly social honeyeaters endemic to Australia. They are known for their complex social organisation, living in large, sedentary colonies that cooperatively defend territories.

children.name: Manorina melanocephala
children.description: A grey bird with a black crown and a bright yellow bill, known for its aggressive, colonial behaviour and loud, discordant calls, abundant in eastern Australia.

children.name: Manorina melanophrys
children.description: A small, olive-green miner with a yellow-orange bill and legs, famous for its distinctive single, bell-like 'tink' call that echoes through the forests of eastern Australia.

children.name: Manorina flavigula
children.description: The yellow-throated miner is a grey honeyeater with a distinctive yellow throat patch and white rump, found across much of Australia in dry woodlands and scrub.

children.name: Manorina melanotis
children.description: The black-eared miner is a grey honeyeater with a distinctive black ear patch and yellow bill, endemic to the mallee woodlands of south-eastern Australia. It is considered endangered.

children.name: Meliphaga
children.description: A genus of stocky, medium-sized honeyeaters with relatively short, broad bills and large yellow ear-patches, found in Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Meliphaga lewinii
children.description: Lewin's honeyeater is a plump, olive-green honeyeater with a prominent yellow ear-patch and a distinctive loud, musical call, found in rainforests and wet eucalypt forests of eastern Australia.

children.name: Meliphaga gracilis
children.description: The graceful honeyeater is a small, slender Meliphaga with a yellow ear-patch and a fine bill, found in lowland forests of Cape York Peninsula and southern New Guinea.

children.name: Meliphaga notata
children.description: The yellow-spotted honeyeater has a distinctive yellow spot behind the eye and a dark ear-patch, endemic to the rainforests of northeastern Queensland.

children.name: Melithreptus
children.description: A genus of small to medium-sized honeyeaters with bare facial skin and short, fine bills, found in forests and woodlands of Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Melithreptus lunatus
children.description: The white-naped honeyeater has a black head with a white nape band and a bare blue eye patch, found in eucalypt forests across eastern and south-western Australia.

children.name: Melithreptus gularis
children.description: The black-chinned honeyeater has a black cap and chin with a white nape and blue bare eye skin, found in dry woodlands across much of Australia.

children.name: Melithreptus brevirostris
children.description: The brown-headed honeyeater has a brown head, white eye-ring, and olive back, found in forests and woodlands across southern and eastern Australia.

children.name: Melithreptus validirostris
children.description: The strong-billed honeyeater has a black head, white nape, and a relatively short, stout bill, endemic to Tasmania where it forages on bark and branches for insects.

children.name: Myzomela
children.description: A genus of small, often brightly coloured honeyeaters with fine, decurved bills, distributed across Australia, New Guinea, Indonesia, and the Pacific Islands.

children.name: Myzomela sanguinolenta
children.description: The scarlet myzomela has brilliant red head and breast with black wings and tail, found in forests and woodlands of eastern Australia.

children.name: Myzomela obscura
children.description: The dusky myzomela has plain brownish-grey plumage with a fine decurved bill, found in lowland forests of northern Australia, New Guinea, and the Moluccas.

children.name: Myzomela erythrocephala
children.description: The red-headed myzomela has a bright red head and breast with olive-brown back, found in mangroves and coastal forests of northern Australia and southern New Guinea.

children.name: Myzomela blasii
children.description: The Papuan myzomela has a red head and breast with olive-grey back, found in lowland forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Philemon
children.description: The friarbirds: a genus of large honeyeaters with bare dark heads and prominent knobbed bills, found in Australia, New Guinea, and the Pacific Islands. They are named for their bald appearance reminiscent of monks.

children.name: Philemon argenticeps
children.description: The silver-crowned friarbird has a silvery-white crown, bare black face, and brown body, found in woodlands and mangroves across northern Australia.

children.name: Philemon corniculatus
children.description: The noisy friarbird has a bare black head, prominent bill knob, and grey-brown body, found in forests and woodlands of eastern Australia. It is named for its loud, raucous calls.

children.name: Philemon citreogularis
children.description: The little friarbird is the smallest friarbird, with a grey-brown body, bare black face, and a yellow throat, found in woodlands across northern and eastern Australia and southern New Guinea.

children.name: Philemon buceroides
children.description: The helmeted friarbird has a bare dark head with a prominent casque-like bill knob, found in forests of the Moluccas, New Guinea, and northern Australia.

children.name: Phylidonyris
children.description: A genus of boldly patterned honeyeaters with white eyebrows and prominent black facial markings, found in coastal and heathland habitats across southern Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Phylidonyris novaehollandiae
children.description: A boldly patterned black-and-white honeyeater with a distinctive white eyebrow, a yellow wing-patch, and a piercing, metallic call, widespread across southern Australia.

children.name: Phylidonyris pyrrhopterus
children.description: The crescent honeyeater has a distinctive black crescent-shaped mark on the breast, with olive-brown plumage and yellow wing patches, found in wet forests and heathlands of south-eastern Australia and Tasmania.

children.name: Phylidonyris niger
children.description: The white-cheeked honeyeater is a black-and-white honeyeater with a prominent white cheek patch and yellow wing panel, found in coastal heathlands and forests of eastern and south-western Australia.

children.name: Prosthemadera
children.description: A monotypic genus endemic to New Zealand, containing the Tui: a large, charismatic honeyeater with distinctive white throat tufts. It has a uniquely ornate, double-voiced songbox that allows it to produce complex, layered vocalisations.

children.name: Prosthemadera novaeseelandiae
children.description: A striking, iridescent green-black honeyeater with a distinctive white tuft at the throat, endemic to New Zealand. Its complex, melodic song incorporates both original notes and mimicry of other birds.

children.name: Ptilotula
children.description: A genus of medium-sized honeyeaters with plain plumage and colourful facial markings, recently split from Lichenostomus, found across Australia.

children.name: Ptilotula plumula
children.description: The grey-fronted honeyeater has a grey forehead, olive back, and yellow underparts, found in dry woodlands and mallee across much of Australia.

children.name: Ptilotula flavescens
children.description: The yellow-tinted honeyeater has a yellow-tinted face and underparts with olive-brown back, found in woodlands and mangroves of northern Australia and southern New Guinea.

children.name: Ptilotula keartlandi
children.description: The grey-headed honeyeater has a grey head, white throat, and yellow underparts, found in dry woodlands and acacia scrub of inland Australia.

children.name: Ramsayornis
children.description: A small genus of honeyeaters with finely streaked underparts, found in northern Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Ramsayornis fasciatus
children.description: The bar-breasted honeyeater has a brown back, white underparts with dark barring on the breast, and a fine decurved bill, found in mangroves and woodlands of northern Australia.

children.name: Xanthotis
children.description: A genus of medium-sized honeyeaters with spotted or streaked plumage, found in Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Xanthotis flaviventer
children.description: The tawny-breasted honeyeater has a brown back, tawny breast, and yellow belly, found in lowland forests of New Guinea and neighbouring islands.

children.name: Xanthotis polygrammus
children.description: The spotted honeyeater has olive-brown plumage with white spots on the breast and throat, found in lowland forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Xanthotis macleayanus
children.description: Macleay's honeyeater has a brown back, streaked breast, and yellow belly, endemic to the rainforests of north-eastern Queensland.
